results.title: journalctl Commands Cheatsheet for Troubleshooting | Last9
results.content: Quickly diagnose and resolve system issues with this journalctl cheat sheet—essential commands for filtering, viewing, and analyzing logs. With systemd, these logs are centralized in the journal, making `journalctl` the primary tool for accessing and analyzing them. Journalctl is the command-line tool that lets you access and filter the systemd journal – basically your system's diary of everything that happens behind the scenes. | `journalctl -b` | Show logs from current boot | `journalctl -b` |. | `journalctl -b -1` | Show logs from previous boot | `journalctl -b -1` |. | `journalctl -u SERVICE` | Show logs for specific service | `journalctl -u nginx` |. | `journalctl -D PATH` | Use journal at specified path | `journalctl -D /var/log/journal/UUID` |. If `journalctl` logs point to out-of-memory issues, this guide explains how the Linux OOM Killer works. Journalctl provides a unified interface to structured binary logs with rich metadata, advanced filtering, and built-in rotation – all from one command.

results.title: System logs and the systemd journal | ServerPilot Documentation
results.content: # System logs and the systemd journal. A server’s system log contains entries created by the Linux kernel. The service manager, `systemd`, also stores a copy of the system logs in a searchable journal. The `journalctl` command can be used to view entries from the journal. ## View the logs. A server’s system logs. The server’s system log is located at:. The format of each entry in the system log is:. The following example shows a log entry generated by the kernel. For entries created by non-kernel components, the process ID that generated the log message is included in brackets (for example, `systemd[1]` or CRON[2545]). You must be logged in as `root` to view the system log. ### View recent system log entries. Show the most recent 100 lines in the system log. Search the current week’s system log for “Out of memory” messages. ### Use `journalctl` to view the system log. Show the most recent 100 entries from the journal.
